en.extract:
The brown-capped rosy finch (Leucosticte australis) is a medium-sized finch endemic to North America in the middle of the Rocky Mountains.
Adults are brown on the head, back, and breast. The belly and rump are pink, with additional pink shading on the wings and tail. The pink is pronounced on the male but more subdued on the female. The forehead is black or grayish brown. The bill is yellow in winter and black in summer. These birds have short black legs and a long forked tail.
Their breeding habitat is mountain peaks in the central Rocky Mountains of the United States. They build a cup nest in a cavity on a cliff, or re-use abandoned cliff swallow nests. In winter, these birds migrate short distances to lower elevations. After breeding, the female takes three days to finish the nest.
These birds can be found in alpine snowfields and also at feeders, especially in the winter. They  forage on the ground, but may fly to catch insects in flight. They mainly eat seeds from weeds and grasses and insects. They often feed in small flocks with other rosy finches.
At one time, the three North American rosy finches were considered to be one species.
Despite fears that the population of this bird is declining, an analysis published in 2023 found a healthy population with more than three times the birds estimated in a 2016 report.

Hendricks P. (1978). Notes on the Courtship Behavior of Brown-Capped Rosy Finches. Wilson Bulletin. vol 90, no 2. pp. 285–287.
Hendricks P. (1980). Reaction of Brown-Capped Rosy Finches Leucosticte-Australis to Banded Nestlings. Journal of Field Ornithology. vol 51, no 2.

The '''brown-capped rosy finch''' ('''''Leucosticte australis''''') is a medium-sized [[finch]] endemic to [[North America]] in the middle of the [[Rocky Mountains]].<ref name=":0">{{Cite web |title=Brown-capped Rosy-Finch Identification, All About Birds, Cornell Lab of Ornithology |url=https://www.allaboutbirds.org/guide/Brown-capped_Rosy-Finch/id |access-date=2025-02-15 |website=www.allaboutbirds.org |language=en}}</ref>

Adults are brown on the head, back, and breast. The belly and rump are pink, with additional pink shading on the wings and tail.<ref name=":0" /><ref name=":4" /> The pink is pronounced on the male but more subdued on the female.<ref name=":0" /> The forehead is black or grayish brown.<ref name=":4">{{Cite web |title=Brown-capped Rosy-Finch - eBird |url=https://ebird.org/ebird/species/bcrfin/ |access-date=2025-02-15 |website=ebird.org |language=en}}</ref> The bill is yellow in winter and black in summer.<ref name=":0" /> These birds have short black legs and a long forked tail.

Their breeding habitat is mountain peaks in the central [[Rocky Mountains]] of the [[United States]].<ref name=":0" /><ref name=":1">{{Cite web |title=Brown-capped Rosy-Finch {{!}} Audubon Field Guide |url=https://www.audubon.org/field-guide/bird/brown-capped-rosy-finch |access-date=2025-02-15 |website=www.audubon.org |language=en}}</ref> They build a cup nest in a cavity on a cliff, or re-use abandoned [[cliff swallow]] nests. In winter, these birds [[bird migration|migrate]] short distances to lower elevations.<ref name=":0" /><ref name=":1" /> After breeding, the female takes three days to finish the nest.

These birds can be found in alpine snowfields and also at feeders, especially in the winter.<ref name=":3">{{Cite web |title=Brown-capped Rosy-Finch - eBird |url=https://ebird.org/ebird/species/bcrfin/ |access-date=2025-02-15 |website=ebird.org |language=en}}</ref> They  forage on the ground, but may fly to catch insects in flight.<ref name=":0" /> They mainly eat seeds from weeds and grasses and insects. They often feed in small flocks with other [[Mountain finch|rosy finch]]es.<ref name=":3" />

At one time, the three North American rosy finches were considered to be one species.<ref name=":2">{{Cite web |last=Rutter |first=Jordan |date=2023-07-06 |title=Demystifying Rosy-Finches: Understanding the American West's Off-the-Grid Endemics |url=https://abcbirds.org/rosy-finches/ |access-date=2025-02-15 |website=American Bird Conservancy |language=en}}</ref>

Despite fears that the population of this bird is declining, an analysis published in 2023 found a healthy population with more than three times the birds estimated in a 2016 report.<ref name=":2" />
